article.title: Venter Institute Scientists Revive Dead Bacteria With Genome Transplants, Creating 'Zombie Cells' That Could Reshape Synthetic Biology
article.summary: A team at the J. Craig Venter Institute has resurrected dead bacterial cells by replacing their destroyed DNA with synthetic genomes, overcoming a longstanding contamination barrier in whole-genome transplantation.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Researchers at the J. Craig Venter Institute (JCVI) have demonstrated a technique for reviving dead bacterial cells by transplanting complete synthetic genomes into them, according to a [Nature news report](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6) covering the work. The team dubbed the resulting organisms "zombie cells" because the host bacteria were chemically killed before receiving a new genome that restored them to life. The findings were [posted on the bioRxiv preprint server](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.03.13.711674v1) on March 14 and have not yet undergone peer review.

The advance addresses a persistent technical barrier in synthetic biology: when researchers attempt to transplant an engineered genome into a living bacterium, the recipient cell's own DNA contaminates the result, making it difficult to determine whether the transplanted genome is truly functioning on its own.

## What We Know

The JCVI team, led by John Glass, treated cells of the bacterium *Mycoplasma capricolum* with mitomycin C, a chemotherapy drug that crosslinks and destroys DNA. This rendered the cells metabolically active but unable to replicate, effectively creating "blank templates" stripped of functional genetic material, as [reported by Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6).

The researchers then used whole-genome transplantation to insert a complete synthetic genome from a related species, *Mycoplasma mycoides*, into these inactivated cells. A small fraction of the treated bacteria resumed growth and division, with genetic testing confirming that the donor genome had taken full control of cellular functions, according to the [bioRxiv preprint](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.03.13.711674v1).

"By taking a cell without a genome and adding a new one, we can resurrect it," Glass said, as [quoted by Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6). Co-author Zumra Peksaglam Seidel, a synthetic biologist at JCVI, described the process more starkly: "The cell is destined to die, but we give it life," according to the same [Nature report](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6).

The technique is described in the preprint as "selection-free" whole-genome transplantation, meaning it eliminates the need for antibiotic resistance markers that previous methods relied on to identify successful transplants. Earlier approaches suffered from false positives caused by homologous recombination, where fragments of the recipient's genome would incorporate antibiotic resistance genes from the donor without actually accepting the full transplant, as described in the [bioRxiv preprint](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.03.13.711674v1).

## What We Don't Know

Several significant uncertainties remain. The process currently works efficiently only between closely related *Mycoplasma* species, and the precise mechanisms underlying successful genome transplantation are still poorly understood, [Nature reports](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6).

Whether the zombie cell approach can be extended to more widely used laboratory organisms such as *Escherichia coli* or yeast remains an open question. These organisms present additional challenges due to their larger genomes and the presence of rigid cell walls that *Mycoplasma* species lack, according to [Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6).

The preprint has not yet been peer-reviewed, meaning independent verification of the results and methodology is still pending.

## Analysis

The zombie cell technique builds on more than 15 years of genome transplantation research at JCVI. The institute first demonstrated whole-genome transplantation in 2007 and created the first self-replicating synthetic bacterial cell, nicknamed Synthia, in 2010. That earlier work proved that a chemically synthesized genome could be "booted up" inside a living cell, but the approach was limited by contamination from the host cell's native DNA.

By killing the recipient cells before transplantation, the JCVI team has effectively sidestepped that contamination problem. If the approach proves generalizable beyond *Mycoplasma*, it could provide a standardized platform for testing AI-designed synthetic genomes, enabling researchers to verify engineered organisms before deploying them in drug manufacturing, biofuel production, or environmental remediation, as [noted by Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6).

Elizabeth Strychalski of the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology, a co-author on the preprint, told [Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00938-6) that the work raises fundamental questions about the boundary between living and non-living systems, noting that life functions as a set of processes rather than a fixed state.
